Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»PROFILE: Inspirational Jordy Smith still riding the crest of a wave

South Africa’s surfing superstar Jordy Smith will turn to his experience in the waves at the Paris Olympics 2024.

Jordy Smithlooks at the cover of the magazine. The underwater image is of a surfer and the headline reads: “He Walks On Water. And surfing’s new god is South African.”

For the latest Paris Olympics 2024 news, bookmarkThe South African website’sdedicated section

That was August 2010. He was 22 years-old. Globally he was already big news, from when he first qualified for the elite ASP World Tour in 2008. Tiger Woods had asked him to sign with Nike.

“I wasn’t sure what to say, but he was cool,” he said at the time.

A few days later he reportedly signed with O’Neil for a seven-figure sum. In Hawaii he stayed with A-list actors like Owen Wilson and Ben Stiller. He was ranked No 1 in the world and had already appeared in surfing movies. You get the picture.

Now, aged 36, he is the oldest surfer on the professional circuit, but is an example that dreams do come true.

“Wow,” he says looking at the magazine.

“So much has happened since then.”

WATCH |Team SA surfing team pack their boards for Tahiti

We’re having a chatin the Bidvest lounge at Cape Town International where Smith and the Team SA surfing team are starting a journey that will take them to Dubai, Los Angeles and finally on to Tahiti.

To be exact their final destination would be the village of Teahupo’o where the 2024 Olympics are being held. The venue was chosen not only because it has big waves, but Tahiti is an island in French Polynesia and it’s part of a wider plan to align France with its territories.

Jordy Smith is a veteran of the world tour, but an Olympic newbie. He should have been in Tokyo where the sport was introduced to the programme, but tore ligaments and had to have reconstructed knee surgery a month before those Games.

Paris Olympics 2024 |Meet South Africa’s SURFING team

“You know, when that happened I thought I’d never represent South Africa at a Games. No one knew how surfing would be received at the Olympics and whether the opportunity would come round again. I never stopped believing though.”

In Tokyo, Bianca Buitendag was one of only two Team SA medallists and did a lot to put surfing back on the map. Obviously Smith wants to deliver more joy and recognition for the sport in South Africa.

“You know, driving to the airport today, it hit me. I’m going to the Olympics for South Africa. The tears started to flow. It’s such an honour representing one’s country and with everything that I’ve been through, to be on the plane is something special.”

Surfing is one of 19 different sports being represented by Team SA at these Games and Smith is joined byMatt McGillivrayandSarah Ann Baumflying the flag on the majestic island some 17 730 kilometres from Paris, separated by a time zone of 12 hours. Some 52 hours 52 minutes and 20 seconds after taking off from Cape Town, they arrived in Tahiti.

However, while Tokyo had smaller waves, Teahupo’o is known for its thunderous sets.

“They’re powerful, great for a big guy like myself. We have to wake up and see what Mother Nature delivers on competition day though.”

In trying to fill in the gaps from 2010 to 2024, Jordy Smith says, “gosh, where do I start?”

How about at a career highlight?

“That’s an easy one. In 2011 I won my first event on the tour in Jeffreys Bay. To do so in front of my family and friends who had been around that town since I started out, will always be a moment I’ll cherish and never forget.”

Since then much has happened.

“I’ve won multiple times on the championship tour, got married, had a kid … lots of amazing things. It’s kinda wild to see that I’m still here, still doing it, still loving it and I’m just super passionate about what I do.”

He splits his time between Cape Town, Jeffrey’s Bay and Hawaii.

“I’ve been going to Hawaii since I was 12 and have a long list of friends there, It’s a nice place to be in their winter time, which is our summer, so we spend about 2-3 months there at the end of the year. For a South African it’s always a fun journey.”

Jordy Smith admits that he has a lot more freedom in South Africa.

“I’ve always thought of SA as a country with three big sports – rugby, cricket and soccer. Surfing is a bit unappreciated but as a sport it’s big on the global stage. It’s massive in places like the US, Brazil and Australia, but what it means is that I can walk around unrecognised at home, whereas that’s not the case in those countries, So it’s fair to say I have the best of both worlds, I can be anonymous in SA and it’s a different way of life.”

His son Ziggy is now three-and-a-half.

“When he was born we decided to name him Ziggy, after Ziggy Stardust and Ziggy Marley. Besides, with a surname like Smith we had to spice it up a bit for him. After all, Smith is quite a plain name! He really loves skateboarding and surfing and it’s strange to see myself in him and at the same time very cool.”

Dad duties aside, Jordy is still highly motivated to surprise the youngsters on the circuit.

“I’m the oldest guy on tour so it’s very motivating and inspiring to be able to compete against guys who are 15, and even 20-years younger. There’s no limit to it – look at Kelly Slater, still competing at the age of 52 – and if you feel you’re good enough to still do it then the only thing left is for you to do it.

“My goal is to inspire South Africa’s future surfing generation. I want my achievements to shed more light on the sport and to show people that surfing can be a professional job. It’s not just these guys hanging on the beach all day and messing around. For me, if I can get involved with schools, charities and organisations and help benefit youngsters then I’ve done a great job.

“I think that one should do whatever one loves, every day and if someone appreciates what you do then good, if not, then good as well. It doesn’t really matter.”

He knows there will be a time when he has to pack away his surfboard, in a professional sense, and move on with the next phase of his life. However, that’s not happening anytime soon.

“Maybe in three to five years? Experience over age can be a great leveller in this sport, so I still have ambitions, starting with these Olympics.

“And I don’t want to be that person who hangs around too long, I want to retire at the top. Plus, in a couple of years my son will be coming into his own.”

Ah, that’s Ziggy of course, Sprinkling his own stardust that dad has done for so many years.

Gary Lemkeis reporting live from Paris for the Team SA website for the duration of the Paris Olympics 2024
